Adds ا at the end with fatha to make it a complete demonstrative pronoun. In this ayah, it's pointing to what the disbelievers used to claim.
Base demonstrative word that points to something
Adding ٱلّ turns this from a pointing word ('this/that') into a connecting word ('who/which'). Like turning 'that' into 'that which' in English
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas in a hollow verb
Changed vowels to make command form - first vowel becomes damma, middle alif drops out
Added تُ at the end to make it 'I was' (not 'you were')
Added م to make it plural 'you all'
Added kasra to the preposition ب to connect it with what follows
Added ه (hi) at the end to mean 'him/it' - like adding 'him' after 'with'

steps.m: with/by/in him/it (referring to what they used to claim)
steps.n: Added a small alif (ۦ) after ه to help with pronunciation. This word refers back to what the disbelievers used to claim or call for.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels
Added ت at beginning to change from 'he' to 'you'
Added shadda to show emphasis and intensity of the claim

steps.m: you all claim/used to claim (referring to the disbelievers in the ayah)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people, and changed the middle vowel to damma because of this ending
Connected the root letters together with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a command form. The ُ on first letter and ْ on last letter show it's telling someone to do something.
The weak letter و is dropped to make the command simpler, which is common in Arabic commands
Changed the root letters into past tense 'he saw' by adding the vowel marks
Added hamza at beginning to make it a question
Added تَ to make it 'you' instead of 'he'
Added plural 'you' ending to show multiple people are being addressed
This is a conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. Here it introduces the condition 'if He destroys me' before the main clause about who will protect the disbelievers.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the beginning and sukun on first root letter changes the meaning from 'to perish' to 'to cause to perish/destroy'
Adding نِي at the end shows that 'me' is the object being destroyed
Adding fatha at the end for proper pronunciation in the Quran
Changed the concept of worship into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
This is the unique name for The One True God in Islam. The ال makes it specific, and the doubled ل and special spelling show this is a unique proper name, not just any deity.
This is a relative pronoun that means 'whoever' or 'who' - it's used to start a condition or question about people
Added و at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it in the ayah
Added basic vowel marks to the base word meaning 'with'
Changed the final vowel to kasra to prepare for the attached pronoun
Added the letter ى at the end to show this means 'me'
Added shadda on first letter for emphasis and fatha at the end for pronunciation
This is a connecting word that shows two possible situations: either Allah destroys them or shows mercy to them
Added vowel marks to make a past tense verb showing completed action
Added نَا at the end to show who received the mercy (us)
This is a question word that asks about people (who) or can mean 'whoever' in conditional sentences
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence. This word is asking who will protect/save, connecting to the verb يُجِيرُ (protects/saves) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense ('he protected' to 'he protects'). The middle letter changed from و to ي. This verb is asking 'who protects?' in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes from 'he rejected' (a verb) to 'one who rejects' (a doer). Like changing 'write' to 'writer'. This word describes someone who doesn't accept the truth.

steps.m: those who reject (truth) (acted upon by يُجِيرُ)
steps.n: Regular masculine plural form. This word is the object (the one receiving the action) of the verb يُجِيرُ (protects/saves).
Added 'the' at the beginning and the accusative case ending because this word is receiving the action of the verb يُجِيرُ (protects/saves)

This is a preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the word عَذَابٍ (punishment) to the verb يُجِيرُ (protect), showing what the disbelievers need protection from.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second letter to make it a noun meaning the result of punishment
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this is an indefinite noun describing what kind of punishment it is - specifically a painful one as shown by the next word أَلِيمٍ
Added vowels and ي to make it into an adjective showing intense quality
Added tanween kasra to show this is describing the punishment (عَذَابٍ) mentioned in the ayah
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: say! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to the disbelievers)
steps.n: Changed to a command form by: 1) Adding damma on first letter, 2) Dropping the middle weak letter و, 3) Adding sukun at the end. This is Allah commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to the disbelievers about the Most Merciful (الرحمن).
This is a pronoun that means 'he'. In this ayah, it refers to who is 'in clear error'. It doesn't come from a root because it's one of the basic building blocks of Arabic.

steps.m: to be merciful, to show kindness and care

steps.n: Creates an intensive form showing complete and perfect mercy

steps.m: extremely merciful and caring (as the main subject)
steps.n: Added ُ at the end to show this word is doing something in the sentence (it's describing 'He' from هُوَ before it)
Added ٱل at start to make it specific (like 'the' in English) and doubled the ر with ّ to emphasize the meaning
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb about feeling safe
Added alif after hamza to change the meaning from just feeling safe to actively believing in something
Added نَّا at the end to show 'we' did this action
Basic preposition that shows how something is done or connected
Added هِ to show who/what the preposition connects to
Added ۦ as a pronunciation helper to show the هِ is connected to what comes after
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation of basic preposition
Changed ending to prepare for attachment of pronoun
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'upon him'
Added وَ at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start and doubled middle letter to show doing the action to/for oneself - putting trust in someone
Changed from 'he put trust' to 'we put trust' by adding نا at the end - this shows a group of people did the action
Basic past tense form meaning 'he knew' - no ت is present in this word
Adding ت changes it from 'he knew' to either 'she knows' or 'you know'
Adding ُ shows this is happening in the present tense
Added س (sa) at start to make it future tense ('will know')
Added ون to make it plural 'you all'
Added فَ to connect this with previous statement, meaning 'then/so'

steps.m: who (asking about the one in ضَلٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ - clear error)
steps.n: This is a question word that asks about people. In this ayah, it's asking who is the one that will be in clear error.

steps.m: he (subject of the sentence, referring to الرَّحْمٰنُ)
steps.n: This is the basic form of the pronoun meaning 'he'

steps.m: he (subject of the sentence, referring to الرَّحْمٰنُ)
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) on ه and fatha (َ) on و to show correct pronunciation
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else
Added the ى at the end which is standard for this preposition. This word connects to ضَلٰلٍ (misguidance) to show being 'in misguidance'
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic noun form
Added alif after second root letter to show intensity of the state of being lost
Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite and in genitive case
The alif (ا) changed to a long alif (ٰ) but keeps the same meaning of misguidance. The kasra marks show it comes after 'in/fee'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'one who' and changed inside vowels to show it's describing something that makes things clear
Added shadda on م to show emphasis

steps.m: one who makes very clear (in error/misguidance)
steps.n: Added tanween (double dots under) to show it connects with another word (ضَلٰلٍ) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

The middle weak letter و is dropped and vowels change to make a command. This is telling one person to speak. This word starts a sentence where Muhammad ﷺ is commanded to ask a question to the disbelievers.
Connected the root letters and added basic past tense vowels. The ى at the end is because this is a special type of word that ends in ي.
Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question
Added ي and changed to plural 'you' form
Added تُمْ to show this is talking to multiple people (you all)
This is a conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. Here it sets up the condition 'if your water becomes sunken' before the main clause.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: it became (something happened to the water in مَآؤُكُمْ)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning and a sukun on the first root letter to create Form IV, which shows entering into a state or becoming something. In this ayah, it's talking about water becoming something.
Changed و to ا because it comes after fatha in this word pattern
Changed ه to hamza (ء) at the end to make the standard spelling of 'water'
The word 'water' gets a 'u' sound at the end (damma) to show it's doing the action in the sentence

steps.m: your water (referring to 'if your water becomes sunken')
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show the water belongs to the people being addressed

steps.m: to sink deep or disappear below surface
sinking deep (describes water in أَصْبَحَ مَآؤُكُمْ)
steps.n: Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make a noun from the basic meaning. This describes how the water (مَآؤُكُمْ) becomes in the phrase 'your water becomes' (أَصْبَحَ مَآؤُكُمْ).

steps.m: sunken deep (describing what happens to the water)
steps.n: Added ًا (tanween) at the end to show this describes how the water becomes - like saying 'the water became deep' instead of just 'deep water'
This is a question word used to ask about people
Added فَ at the beginning to show this question follows from what was said before. This word is asking about who can do the action in يَأْتِيكُم (bring to you).
Connected the root letters together
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, with ْ and ِ marks to complete the present tense form

steps.m: he comes/arrives to you all (addressing multiple people)
steps.n: Added كُم at the end to show the action is directed at multiple people being addressed
The root letters change to make the simple word 'water'. The middle and last letters (و and ه) combine into a hamza (ء) at the end - this is a common way Arabic makes some basic nouns.
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's a long vowel
The ٍ (tanween kasra) at the end shows this is 'some water' that something is happening to

steps.m: with water (connected to يَأْتِيكُم - brings to you)
steps.n: The بِ at the beginning means 'with'. This word connects to يَأْتِيكُم (brings to you) in the ayah, showing what is being brought.

steps.m: to flow or spring forth (like water)

steps.n: Changed from basic root meaning 'to flow' into a word describing something that is flowing, by adding vowel marks
Added مَ at the beginning to make it mean 'a flowing spring/water source' - like turning 'flow' into 'flowing spring'
Added emphasis to show this is definitely flowing

steps.m: flowing water (being described in this ayah)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this describes the water mentioned earlier in the ayah
